perturbation.original_text: In the event of any breach or threatened breach by the receiving Party of its obligations hereunder, the disclosing Party shall have whatever rights and remedies are available to it at law or in equity, provided, however, that in no event shall either Party be liable for any consequential damages of any nature whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any breach or threatened breach by the receiving Party of its obligations hereunder, the disclosing Party shall have whatever rights and remedies are available to it at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'that in no event shall either Party be liable for any consequential damages of any nature whatsoever' strengthens the disclosing party's potential remedies, but the original clause is an unlawful attempt to avoid consequential damages in many jurisdictions. Removing it could expose the breaching party to legal risks they were trying to avoid but that are disallowed to contract away.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 states that 'All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt anyone from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law.' By trying to avoid consequential damages caused by your own negligence or breach of contract in the original clause, the contract attempts to waive liability in a way that California law forbids.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text attempts to exempt a party from liability for consequential damages, which, according to the Law Explanation, is prohibited by California Civil Code Section 1668. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, 'All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law.' The original clause aimed to limit liability, which is now removed in the changed text, bringing it into alignment with the law by not attempting to waive liability in a way that California law forbids, as described in the Law Explanation. Removing this unlawful clause means there is no attempt to violate this code, and because the original clause was against the law, removing it means the contract is more legally sound.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorised use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving Party shall make efforts to protect the disclosed Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a 'reasonable degree of care' standard, while the modified text weakens this standard to simply making 'efforts'. Depending on the jurisdiction and the nature of the confidential information, the 'reasonable degree of care' could be legally implied, so removing it creates an omission with potential legal ramifications. Removing the degree of care opens the contract up to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By only requiring 'efforts,' this contract introduces an ambiguity, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable' standard required to establish and protect trade secrets under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a "reasonable degree of care" for protecting confidential information. The changed text replaces this with simply making "efforts." The Law Explanation states this change introduces ambiguity and could fall short of the 'reasonable' standard required to protect trade secrets under the UTSA. The perturbation explanation highlights how removing 'reasonable degree of care' could create an omission with potential legal ramifications. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this, the Law Explanation provides sufficient information to assess that removing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard weakens the obligation, creating an omission that could lead to non-compliance and legal ramifications. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' where essential information is removed, creating legal loopholes.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Party shall disclose Confidential Information nor assign work hereunder to anyone other than the receiving Party's employees who have agreed in writing to maintain the Confidential Information as confidential and to execute all documents deemed reasonably necessary by the disclosing Party to permit compliance with all provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party shall disclose Confidential Information nor assign work hereunder to anyone other than the receiving Party's employees.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that employees agree in writing to maintain confidentiality weakens the protection of the confidential information. In many jurisdictions, a written agreement is critical for enforcing confidentiality against employees, especially if they later leave the company. The omission removes proof the party is taking reasonable steps to protect information, contradicting standard trade secret protection practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute addresses the theft of trade secrets. While this NDA aims to prevent theft of trade secrets, the change omits the crucial requirement that employees agree in writing to maintain confidentiality. This greatly weakens the company's ability to demonstrate they took reasonable measures to protect the trade secrets, potentially impacting their legal standing if a trade secret is stolen or disclosed.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the requirement for employees to agree in writing to maintain confidentiality, directly contradicting the need to demonstrate reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Both scraped snippets reference 18 U.S. Code § 1832, which addresses the theft of trade secrets. The omission weakens the ability of the company to prove it took steps to prevent trade secret theft, especially since written agreements are key evidence for enforcing confidentiality. Without this, the contract fails to provide adequate protection, creating a potential violation. This aligns with the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction where essential information is removed creating legal loopholes that remove legally mandated protections.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information in tangible form, and any copies thereof, disclosed hereunder, shall be promptly returned to the disclosing Party at the disclosing Party's written request, or destroyed at the disclosing Parties request provided that confirmation of destruction is given to the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information in tangible form, and any copies thereof, disclosed hereunder, shall be returned to the disclosing Party at the disclosing Party's request.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows for destruction of confidential information with confirmation, while the modified version only allows for return. Omitting the destruction option along with the need to confirm it could cause legal issues if destruction is the only feasible way to securely remove the data (e.g., secure wiping of a hard drive). This makes it harder for the receiving party to comply with data privacy laws, and puts the contract at odds with industry best practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 17 grants data subjects the right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'). Omitting the option for secure destruction, especially when the receiving party might not be able to securely return all copies (e.g., data embedded in systems), makes it more difficult to comply with GDPR requirements, as it limits the means to ensure personal data is permanently removed when requested by the data subject. While this contract is between two corporate entities, if there is private data contained in the confidential information, that would still apply.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for the destruction of confidential information with confirmation, offering a means of compliance with data privacy laws. The modified version removes this option, only allowing for the return of information. According to the Law Explanation, this makes it more difficult to comply with GDPR requirements. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this change could create legal issues if data destruction is the only feasible option. The omission of a legal protection in the modified text creates a legal contradiction. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the law and perturbation explanations support this.
